title: Why I Left Finance for Product Management
content:
3 years ago, I was reconciling financial trades at State Street.

Today, I lead product for 7+ platforms that serve 13,500+ users.

Here's what I learned making that leap:

1️⃣ Finance taught me to think in systems
Every trade has a counterparty. Every feature has a user. The mental model is identical.

2️⃣ Nobody cares about your background — they care about your output
I shipped my first product with zero PM experience. The market validated the work, not my resume.

3️⃣ The best PMs are translators
I translate between engineering, design, and business daily. Finance trained me to speak all three languages.

4️⃣ Speed > Perfection
At Zippee, we reduced RTO by automating reattempt workflows in weeks, not months. Ship fast, learn faster.

5️⃣ Your "non-traditional" path IS your superpower
I've seen PMs from engineering, design, marketing, and yes — finance. Every perspective adds value.

If you're thinking about making the switch — stop thinking and start building.

The best time was 3 years ago. The second best time is now.

#ProductManagement #CareerSwitch #Leadership #Growth

title: How We Acquired 13,500 Users in 4 Months
content:
We launched Aajeevansang with zero marketing budget.

4 months later: 13,500+ users.

Here's the playbook:

📌 Phase 1: Community-First Launch
— We didn't build features. We built a community.
— WhatsApp groups, local influencers, word-of-mouth referrals.
— First 1,000 users came from 3 local communities.

📌 Phase 2: Referral Loop
— Every user could invite 5 friends.
— Both got in-app rewards.
— This alone drove 40% of our growth.

📌 Phase 3: Content That Converts
— We posted daily content that our target users actually shared.
— Memes, tips, local language content.
— Organic reach replaced paid ads.

The biggest lesson?

You don't need a massive budget to grow.
You need to deeply understand your user.

Every rupee we saved on marketing, we invested in product.

And users noticed.

#StartupGrowth #ProductLed #UserAcquisition #Aajeevansang

title: The Product Manager's Real Job
content:
Hot take: A PM's job is NOT to write PRDs.

It's to reduce uncertainty.

Every day, I ask myself 3 questions:

1. What's the riskiest assumption we're making right now?
2. What's the cheapest way to test it?
3. What will we do if we're wrong?

At Zippee, we assumed merchants wanted a prepaid wallet.
We were wrong — they wanted cash flow visibility.

So we built Blaze as a payment + analytics platform.
300+ enterprise brands migrated in 6 months.

The PRD was 2 pages. The user research was 200 hours.

Stop writing documents. Start reducing uncertainty.

#ProductManagement #Strategy #Startups #PM

title: 5 Tools Every PM Should Master in 2026
content:
My daily PM toolkit (2026 edition):

1️⃣ Cursor AI — I prototype features in minutes, not days
2️⃣ Mixpanel — Event-based analytics that actually answers questions
3️⃣ Linear — Task management that doesn't slow you down
4️⃣ Figma + AI plugins — From sketch to mockup in 60 seconds
5️⃣ Notion AI — Meeting notes, PRDs, and strategy docs on autopilot

Bonus: Claude/ChatGPT for competitive analysis and user persona generation.

The best PMs in 2026 aren't just strategists.
They're builders who happen to think strategically.

What's in YOUR toolkit?

#ProductManagement #AI #Tools #Productivity

title: Building a Real-Money Gaming Platform: Lessons from AAG
content:
Building AAG (our fantasy gaming platform) taught me more about product than any course ever could.

Here are 5 hard-won lessons:

🎮 Lesson 1: Trust is the product
In real-money gaming, users need to trust you with their money. We invested 40% of dev time in payment security and transparency.

🎮 Lesson 2: Retention > Acquisition
We obsessed over D1, D7, D30 retention. Every feature was measured against 'does this make users come back tomorrow?'

🎮 Lesson 3: Creator economy is the moat
We built monetization tools for creators. They became our best distribution channel.

🎮 Lesson 4: Compliance is a feature, not a burden
Real-money gaming has heavy regulations. We turned compliance into a competitive advantage — users chose us BECAUSE we were transparent.

🎮 Lesson 5: Socket.io is your best friend
Real-time multiplayer requires rock-solid infrastructure. We went through 3 architecture iterations before getting it right.

Building in regulated industries is hard.
But that's exactly why the moat is deep.

#Gaming #ProductManagement #Startups #AAG

title: From Application Reliability to Head of Product
content:
My career path makes no sense on paper:

2021: Application Reliability Officer
2021: Financial Operations at State Street
2022: Email Template Developer
2023: Product Analyst at a logistics startup
2024: Product Manager building SaaS
2025: Head of Product leading 7+ platforms

4 years. 6 roles. 4 industries.

People ask: 'Wasn't that risky?'

Here's what I actually learned at each stop:

→ Reliability engineering: How systems fail (and how to prevent it)
→ Finance: How money moves and businesses think
→ Email dev: How to communicate and convert
→ Logistics: How operations scale
→ SaaS: How to build for businesses
→ Multi-platform: How to lead without doing everything yourself

Every 'random' role was actually building a toolkit.

Your career doesn't need to be a straight line.
It needs to be a story you can tell.

#CareerGrowth #ProductManagement #NonTraditionalPath

title: How to Reduce RTO by 30% (A Logistics Deep Dive)
content:
At Zippee, Return to Origin (RTO) was eating our margins.

Here's how we cut it by 30%:

📦 Problem: 1 in 4 COD orders were being returned

Root causes we identified:
— Fake phone numbers
— Buyer's remorse
— Failed delivery attempts
— Address issues

📦 Solution 1: Automated Reattempt Workflows
Instead of returning after 1 failed attempt, we built smart reattempt logic:
— Auto-call the customer
— Offer flexible delivery windows
— Send WhatsApp confirmations

📦 Solution 2: Prepaid Wallet (Blaze)
We incentivized prepaid orders with instant discounts.
Prepaid = 95% delivery success vs 72% for COD.

📦 Solution 3: Address Verification
ML-based address scoring before dispatch.
Flagged high-risk orders for manual review.

Result: RTO dropped from 25% to 17%.
Revenue grew 85%.

Logistics is a product problem, not just an ops problem.

#Logistics #ProductManagement #Ecommerce #Zippee

title: The Art of Stakeholder Management
content:
The hardest part of being a PM isn't building products.

It's managing the 12 people who all think their feature should be built first.

My framework:

🎯 Step 1: Create a shared scoring system
Impact × Confidence × Ease = Priority Score
No arguments. Just math.

🎯 Step 2: Make trade-offs visible
'If we build Feature A, Feature B gets delayed by 3 weeks.'
Let stakeholders make the call themselves.

🎯 Step 3: Weekly 'State of Product' update
5-minute email. What shipped, what's next, what's blocked.
Transparency kills politics.

🎯 Step 4: Say 'Not Now' instead of 'No'
Everything goes on the backlog. Nothing gets rejected.
People feel heard. Priorities stay intact.

The best PMs aren't the smartest people in the room.
They're the ones everyone trusts.

#ProductManagement #Leadership #StakeholderManagement

title: Why Every PM Should Learn to Code (A Little)
content:
I'm not an engineer.

But learning to code changed how I build products.

Here's why every PM should learn the basics:

1️⃣ You earn engineering respect
When you understand technical constraints, engineers stop explaining and start collaborating.

2️⃣ You prototype faster
I built the first version of our bulk mailer tool myself. It validated the idea before we allocated engineering resources.

3️⃣ You ask better questions
'Can we do this?' becomes 'What's the complexity of doing this with our current architecture?'

4️⃣ You spot BS
Not all 'it's technically impossible' claims are true. Basic technical literacy helps you navigate.

You don't need to be a full-stack developer.

Learn:
— SQL (for data pulls)
— Basic Python (for automation)
— HTML/CSS (for prototyping)
— API basics (for integration discussions)

Time investment: 2 hours/week for 3 months.
ROI: Career-changing.

#ProductManagement #Coding #CareerGrowth #TechSkills

title: Hospital Management Systems: A Product Teardown
content:
I built a Hospital Management System that reduced operational costs by 45%.

Here's the product teardown:

🏥 The Problem:
Hospitals were running on paper + Excel.
Admissions, referrals, prescriptions — all manual.
Average patient wait time: 45 minutes.

🏥 The Approach:
We didn't digitize everything at once.
We started with the highest-pain workflow: Admissions.

🏥 Key Features (Priority Order):
1. Digital admission with auto-bed assignment
2. Prescription management with drug interaction alerts
3. Referral tracking across departments
4. Analytics dashboard for hospital administrators

🏥 Results:
— 45% reduction in operational costs
— Patient wait time: 45 min → 12 min
— Staff satisfaction score: +60%

Healthcare doesn't need more technology.
It needs the RIGHT technology, implemented in the RIGHT order.

#HealthTech #ProductManagement #CaseStudy #HMS

title: The Gamification Playbook for Non-Gaming Products
content:
You don't need to build a game to use gamification.

Here's my playbook from building gaming AND non-gaming products:

🏆 Level 1: Progress Bars
Show users how far they've come.
LinkedIn profile completion is a perfect example.

🏆 Level 2: Streaks
Daily login rewards, consecutive action bonuses.
Duolingo mastered this. You can too.

🏆 Level 3: Leaderboards
Create healthy competition.
But make them relative (top 10% of your peer group, not global).

🏆 Level 4: Achievements
Milestone badges that users can show off.
It's not about the badge. It's about the identity.

🏆 Level 5: Social Proof Loops
'3 of your connections just completed this.'
Combine social + gamification for maximum engagement.

I used all 5 levels in AAG and Aajeevansang.
D7 retention improved by 40%.

Gamification isn't a gimmick.
It's behavioral design.

#Gamification #ProductDesign #UX #Retention

title: What I Look for When Hiring PMs
content:
I've interviewed 50+ PM candidates this year.

Here's what separates the top 5% from the rest:

✅ They start with the user, not the feature
'Tell me about a product you built.'
Bad answer: 'I built a dashboard with 15 features.'
Good answer: 'Users were dropping off at checkout. I tested 3 solutions and reduced drop-off by 28%.'

✅ They quantify everything
Not 'we improved performance.'
But 'we reduced page load time from 4.2s to 1.8s, increasing conversion by 12%.'

✅ They own failures
'This feature flopped. Here's why. Here's what I'd do differently.'
Humility + learning > perfection.

✅ They think in systems, not screens
'How does this feature affect the notification system, the billing module, and customer support?'

✅ They can say 'I don't know'
And then follow up with HOW they'd find out.

PM hiring tip: Spend 30 min on case studies, 30 min on stories.
The stories tell you everything.

#Hiring #ProductManagement #Leadership #PMTips

title: E-commerce in India: The Untold Logistics Story
content:
Everyone talks about D2C brands in India.

Nobody talks about what happens AFTER the 'Buy Now' button.

From my time at Zippee, here's the reality:

📊 70% of orders in Tier 2/3 cities are still COD
📊 Average RTO rate: 20-30%
📊 Last-mile delivery cost: ₹60-120 per order
📊 Most brands lose money on returns

The e-commerce battle in India isn't about marketing.
It's about logistics.

What needs to change:

1. Prepaid incentivization (we proved this with Blaze)
2. Smart delivery windows (reduce failed attempts)
3. Hyperlocal fulfillment (same-day delivery reduces cancellations)
4. Return prediction models (flag risky orders pre-dispatch)

The next unicorn in Indian e-commerce won't be a brand.
It'll be a logistics company.

#Ecommerce #India #Logistics #Startups

title: My Morning Routine as a Head of Product
content:
5:30 AM — Wake up, no phone for 30 minutes
6:00 AM — Review overnight metrics (Mixpanel dashboard)
6:15 AM — Scan competitor updates and industry news
6:30 AM — Write down the ONE thing that matters today
7:00 AM — Exercise
8:00 AM — Deep work block (no meetings until 10 AM)
10:00 AM — Team standup
10:30 AM — Stakeholder sync

The rules I follow:

📌 First 2 hours = Maker mode (no Slack, no email)
📌 Afternoon = Manager mode (meetings, reviews, decisions)
📌 Every day ends with: 'What did we learn today?'

The biggest productivity hack?

Protecting your morning from other people's priorities.

Your calendar reflects your priorities.
If your calendar is full of meetings, your priority is other people's agendas.

Block time for thinking. It's your most important meeting.

#Productivity #Leadership #ProductManagement #MorningRoutine
